mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies lifecycle management tasks such as deployment, upgrades, and backups, making it ideal for teams looking to streamline their database operations. Key trade-offs include the need for proper configuration and the complexity of managing custom resources.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator easily.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator using Helm in a dedicated namespace.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace --version 9.0.0

```

> Installs the MySQL Operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Operator Installation
steps.description:
After installation, verify that the MySQL Operator is running correctly in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Check the deployment status of the MySQL Operator.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
Create a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is required for managing the MySQL InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> This secret will be referenced in the InnoDB Cluster configuration.

steps.title: Define MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
Define a MySQL InnoDB Cluster custom resource that utilizes the previously created secret.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

```

> This command creates the InnoDB Cluster using the specified version.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

steps.title: Verify InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
Check the status of the MySQL InnoDB Cluster to ensure it is running as expected.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator

```

> This command lists the InnoDB Clusters in the mysql-operator namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if not properly backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> Uninstalls the MySQL Operator from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This step will upgrade the MySQL Operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o yaml > backup-innodbclusters.yaml

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --version 9.0.1

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup existing InnoDB Cluster configurations.
> Upgrades the MySQL Operator to the specified version.
> Verify the deployment status after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator -o yaml > backup-innodbclusters.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The secret containing MySQL credentials may not be correctly configured.

**Fix:**
Verify the secret with `kubectl get secret mypwds -o yaml` and ensure the credentials are correct.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL Operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator deployment may not be running.

**Fix:**
Check the deployment status with `kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator` and redeploy if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule MySQL pods'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources allocated.

**Fix:**
Ensure the cluster meets the resource requirements (minimum 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM per node).

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may be in the process of initializing or there may be a configuration issue.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the MySQL pods with `kubectl logs <pod-name>` for more details.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.0.30, improved handling of TLS configurations.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 8.4, ensure that your InnoDB Cluster configurations are compatible with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the InnoDB Cluster listed with a status of 'Ready' when running `kubectl get innodbclusters -n mysql-operator`.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.30'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the resource requirements and that you have the necessary tools installed.
